### 4. The Multipolar / Governance Realist

::: aside
**META:** The archetype that most defamiliarizes the debate for a standard LW reader. Critch has 85% p(doom) but not because of a sovereign optimizer. This should surprise and then make sense. The portrait should be about the group, not just Critch — Toner, Russell, Ord each bring a different angle on the same underlying structure.
:::

*Even well-intentioned, individually rational actors can produce catastrophic outcomes together. No rogue optimizer required.*

**What this group believes:** The dominant threat is not one misaligned system achieving strategic dominance. It is the interaction of many systems, labs, states, and competitive incentives in a world where coordination is hard and racing is cheap. Catastrophe arrives not from a single point of failure but from structural dynamics — unsupervised competition, bargaining failures between actors with misaligned interests, and the gradual erosion of meaningful human control without any single decision point where "we could have stopped this."

**The cluster includes voices with quite different entry points:**

- **Andrew Critch** approaches it from formal AI safety and game theory: his [ARCHES framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.04948) (2020) introduced multipolar challenges as analytically distinct from single-agent alignment failure. His p(doom) is ~85%, but his most probable failure path is what he calls "industrial dehumanization" — a slow structural process by which humans lose economic and political relevance in a machine economy, not a dramatic takeover.

- **Helen Toner** ([Georgetown CSET](https://cset.georgetown.edu)) approaches it from strategic studies: the risk landscape is shaped by how labs, states, and militaries interact under competitive pressure. Her concern is less about any system's intrinsic properties and more about the deployment environment — who controls frontier AI, under what rules, and with what incentives to cut corners.

- **Stuart Russell** frames it as an arms race problem: individually rational actors cannot unilaterally slow down, even if each would prefer a world where everyone did. His book *[Human Compatible](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616)* (2019) is the readable entry point.

- **Toby Ord** identifies four distinct AI risk categories in *[The Precipice](https://theprecipice.com/)* (2020) — alignment failures, gradual disempowerment, AI-fueled coups, and AI-enabled WMD — and explicitly links race dynamics to the amplification of all four.

The race dynamic isn't hypothetical. It's described on the record. [Sam Altman has said](https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/sam-altman-says-hes-working-on-the-most-dangerous-technology-in-history-1e5a96a3) he believes he's building one of the most dangerous technologies in human history and is doing it anyway.^[WSJ interview, Jan 2024.] Anthropic's founding documents describe the company as a ["calculated bet"](https://www.anthropic.com/index/core-views-on-ai-safety) — if powerful AI is coming regardless, better for safety-focused labs to be at the frontier. Demis Hassabis has made structurally similar arguments for DeepMind. Each party has roughly the same logic: we'd prefer a world where this development is slower and more careful, but we cannot unilaterally create that world, and so here we are. This is a collectively irrational outcome produced by individually rational reasoning — exactly what the Multipolar Realist's causal model predicts.

What makes this dynamic particularly stable is that the "race as safety bet" argument is coherent on its own terms. If you believe (a) that transformative AI is coming regardless of what any one actor does, and (b) that safety-focused leadership at the frontier produces better outcomes than ceding it to less cautious actors, then racing is not just excusable but obligatory. The argument has real force. The problem is that it's self-reinforcing: everyone who participates has the same justification, which means the collective outcome — many capable actors each racing on safety grounds — is identical to an unconstrained race. The belief that "our presence makes it safer" is very difficult to falsify while you're inside the system. This is the crux that distinguishes the Multipolar Realist from the other archetypes: the question isn't whether the labs are well-intentioned, but whether well-intentioned competitive participation produces better expected outcomes than some form of coordination that doesn't currently exist.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Distributed / competitive: unsafe racing, coordination failure, gradual structural lock-in |
| **Epistemological stance** | Political-economy-first and empirically-conditioned: the relevant evidence is institutional, geopolitical, and game-theoretic |
| **What they reject** | That technical alignment of individual systems is the primary bottleneck; that catastrophe requires a single dramatic threshold event; that governance is downstream of technical facts |
| **What would update them** | Robust international coordination mechanisms that durably slow racing dynamics; evidence that competitive incentives can be restructured without hegemonic enforcement |

**The defamiliarizing observation:** Critch's 85% p(doom) and Yudkowsky's ~99% look numerically similar. They are almost entirely different claims. Yudkowsky worries about one system winning; Critch worries about no one winning, in a world where many systems and actors are all optimizing simultaneously and the resulting dynamics are nobody's plan. These are different causal models that happen to produce similarly bleak expected outcomes and yet imply very different interventions.
